ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Thai basil pork rice with egg; A0FJP (rice-based dish) is the closest defensible broader category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Thai basil pork rice with egg as served in a hawker centre or casual restaurant typically weighs approximately 380 g, comprising a mound of white rice topped with stir-fried minced pork in holy basil sauce and one fried egg.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Basil Pork Rice (with Egg) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Thai basil pork rice (with egg), CR ID F05100144. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this exact entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai basil pork rice with egg as a composite dish. GI estimated at 72 (high) based on the dominant white rice component, which has a well-established GI of approximately 72 per the University of Sydney GI Database (Jasmine rice, cooked). The protein from pork and egg and the fat from cooking oil modestly attenuate the glycaemic response, but the refined white rice base dominates at typical serving proportions. Glycaemic load calculated as: GI (72) × available carbohydrate per serving (16.8 g/100g × 380 g serving / 100) = 72 × 28.5 / 100 ≈ 20.5 g per 380 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 225 mg/100 g; a 380 g serving delivers approximately 855 mg sodium with minimal potassium or fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced fish sauce and oyster sauce when ordering, as a standard 380 g serving contributes approximately 37% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) with low dietary fibre (0.69 g/100 g) produces a high glycaemic load of approximately 20.5 g per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or reduce portion size, as the refined rice base dominates glycaemic load and the low fibre content (0.69 g/100 g) provides insufficient glycaemic buffering despite the protein contribution from pork and egg.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork and fried egg combined with borderline sodium (225 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre (0.69 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in reduced portions, requesting less oil during cooking and considering a poached egg instead of fried to lower saturated fat and AGE intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6) that amplifies inflammatory pathways; fried egg adds advanced glycation end-products (AGEs)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute pork with chicken or tofu, consider a poached egg to reduce AGE formation, and be aware that chilli (a nightshade) may trigger individual flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Basil Pork Rice with Egg (ข้าวผัดกะเพราหมูไข่ดาว, Khao Phat Kaphrao Mu Khai Dao) is a popular Thai street-food and hawker dish consisting of stir-fried minced or sliced pork seasoned with Thai holy basil (kraphao), fresh chilli, garlic, fish sauce, and oyster sauce, served over steamed white jasmine rice and topped with a crispy-edged fried egg. The dish is characterised by its bold, savoury, slightly spicy flavour profile with aromatic herbal notes from the holy basil. The pork filling is cooked at high heat in a wok, producing a lightly caramelised, umami-rich sauce that coats the meat. Per 100 g, the dish provides 151 kcal, 7.58 g protein, 5.72 g fat, and 16.8 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 225 mg. The high-GI white rice base (GI ~72) and low dietary fibre (0.69 g/100 g) make this dish a moderate-to-high glycaemic load meal at standard serving sizes (~380 g). Clinically, it is unsuitable for uncontrolled type 2 diabetes due to the refined rice base, borderline for hypertension due to sodium from fish sauce and oyster sauce, and of concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from pork and frying. It is widely available in Thai restaurants, hawker centres across Singapore, and food courts throughout Southeast Asia. The dish is also known as Pad Krapao Moo Kai Dao and is considered one of Thailand's national dishes.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05100144) at confidence 0.95. The dish is an exact match in the HPB database. GI is estimated from the dominant white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the standard analogous approach for composite rice dishes.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish as of 2026. Saturated_fat_g estimated at 1.8 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for a comparable stir-fried pork and rice dish (USDA SR Legacy), as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry; this is an adapted value and should be verified against a direct HPB saturated fat measurement if available. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on the white jasmine rice component per the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. The dish is Thai cuisine; mandatory Thai-script name (ข้าวผัดกะเพราหมูไข่ดาว) is provided. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable for this Thai dish and have been left null. Halal status is not confirmed as fish sauce and oyster sauce brands vary; consumers should verify halal certification.
